message.content:
{
  "materials": {
    "ppt": {
      "templateId": "visual-focus",
      "templateName": "Visual Focus Classroom Template",
      "themeId": "cbse-academic-blue",
      "title": "Life Processes - Session 1",
      "presentationTitle": "Understanding Life Processes: Nutrition and Respiration",
      "presentationGoal": "To enable students to define life processes, distinguish between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition, and explain the basic steps of respiration.",
      "audience": "Grade 7-9 Science Students",
      "theme": "cbse-academic-blue",
      "themeTokens": {
        "fonts": {
          "heading": "Roboto Slab",
          "body": "Open Sans"
        },
        "colors": {
          "primary": "#1a5f7a",
          "secondary": "#48cae4",
          "accent": "#ff9e00",
          "background": "#ffffff",
          "surface": "#f0f8fa",
          "text": "#2d3436",
          "mutedText": "#636e72"
        },
        "visualStyle": {
          "topBarStyle": "solid-primary",
          "cardStyle": "rounded-elevation",
          "visualFrameStyle": "clean-border-accent"
        }
      },
      "assetSearchPlan": {
        "preferredSources": [
          "Wikimedia Commons",
          "Unsplash Education Collection"
        ],
        "safeSearch": true,
        "licensingNotes": [
          "All images must be CC0 or Creative Commons Attribution compatible for educational use.",
          "No copyrighted textbook diagrams; recreate essential biological structures as original SVGs."
        ],
        "fallbackStrategy": "Use high-contrast schematic line drawings generated via SVG when specific photographic references are unavailable."
      },
      "licenseChecklist": [
        "Verify all external image sources allow educational redistribution.",
        "Ensure no student faces or identifiable individuals appear in generic stock photos without consent flags.",
        "Confirm scientific accuracy of biological diagrams against standard curriculum texts."
      ],
      "presentationWarnings": [
        "Do not introduce cellular respiration details (Krebs cycle) as they are reserved for future sessions.",
        "Avoid discussing complex chemical formulas beyond the basic word equation for photosynthesis and aerobic respiration.",
        "Ensure all biological diagrams clearly label parts without overcrowding."
      ],
      "coverageSummary": {
        "learningOutcomesCovered": [
          "Define life processes as essential functions maintaining living organisms.",
          "Differentiate between autotrophic (self-feeding) and heterotrophic (other-feeding) nutrition.",
          "Explain the basic steps of respiration: inhalation, gas exchange, exhalation.",
          "Identify why all living things need energy."
        ],
        "topicsCovered": [
          "Introduction to Life Processes",
          "Nutrition Types (Autotrophic vs Heterotrophic)",
          "Respiration Mechanism"
        ],
        "taughtConceptsCovered": [
          "Metabolism basics",
          "Photosynthesis overview",
          "Aerobic respiration concept",
          "Role of oxygen and carbon dioxide"
        ],
        "omittedContent": [
          "Detailed chemical equations with stoichiometry.",
          "Anaerobic fermentation processes in depth.",
          "Cellular organelle functions (mitochondria/chloroplasts) beyond basic context."
        ]
      },
      "slides": [
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "title_identity",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Session Title Slide",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 1,
          "slideType": "title_identity",
          "slideTitle": "Life Processes: The Engine of Life",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Intro"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Introduction to Life Processes"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Set the stage for the lesson by defining what 'life processes' are and why they matter.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students understand that life is defined by continuous biological activities like nutrition, respiration, etc.",
          "layout": "hero-title",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "What makes something alive?",
            "The essential functions of living organisms",
            "Today's Focus: Nutrition & Respiration"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Life Processes",
            "Session 1: The Basics of Survival",
            "Nutrition | Respiration"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Introduce the chapter and hook student curiosity about what keeps us alive.",
            "Explain: Start by asking, 'If you stop eating or breathing for a few days, what happens?' Use this to define life processes as activities that maintain our bodies.",
            "Ask: 'Can a rock eat? Can it breathe?' Contrast non-living vs. living things.",
            "Listen for: Student answers like 'growing', 'moving', 'eating'.",
            "Transition: We will look at two critical processes today to understand how we get energy."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "High-quality hero image of a diverse ecosystem (e.g., a forest canopy or underwater scene) with vibrant green and blue tones, symbolizing life. The composition should be clean with negative space for the title text overlay.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Hero visual to establish theme",
              "searchQuery": "vibrant lush forest canopy sunlight dappled leaves educational illustration style high contrast",
              "sourceSite": "Unsplash Education Collection",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC0 / Educational Use",
              "attributionText": "Image for educational purposes only.",
              "altText": "Sunlight filtering through a dense green forest canopy, symbolizing life and photosynthesis.",
              "placementHint": "Full background with dark overlay to ensure text readability."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in title from top",
            "Bullet points appear sequentially on click"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 2
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "core_concept",
          "templateSlideTitle": "What are Life Processes?",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 2,
          "slideType": "core_concept",
          "slideTitle": "Defining Life Processes",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Definition"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Introduction to Life Processes"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Provide a clear, concise definition of life processes and list the key examples.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students can define 'life processes' as continuous biological activities required for survival.",
          "layout": "split-content",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Continuous vital functions in living organisms",
            "Examples: Nutrition, Respiration, Transportation, Excretion",
            "Without these, life ceases to exist"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Life Processes = Essential Functions",
            "- Nutrition (Getting Food)",
            "- Respiration (Releasing Energy)",
            "- Transportation (Moving Nutrients)",
            "- Excretion (Removing Waste)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Define the term 'life processes' explicitly.",
            "Explain: These are not one-time events but continuous activities. Just like a car needs fuel and exhaust removal, living things need these functions constantly.",
            "Ask: 'Which of these do you think is most important right now?' (Respiration).",
            "Correct: If students say only eating matters, clarify that breathing happens every second even while sleeping.",
            "Board work: Write the four main processes on the board as a checklist."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Clean infographic style layout. On one side, text bullets; on the other, a simple icon set representing each process (e.g., an apple for nutrition, lungs for respiration). Use flat design icons in primary blue and accent orange.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Iconography to reinforce concepts",
              "searchQuery": "flat vector icons food breathing transport waste removal educational set minimal style white background",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons (Public Domain)",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC0 / Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Icons adapted for classroom use.",
              "altText": "Four simple icons representing nutrition, respiration, transportation, and excretion.",
              "placementHint": "Right column of the slide layout."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Icons appear one by one as each process is mentioned"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "worked_example",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Types of Nutrition: Autotrophic vs Heterotrophic",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 3,
          "slideType": "comparison",
          "slideTitle": "How Organisms Get Food",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Nutrition"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Nutrition Types (Autotrophic vs Heterotrophic)"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Distinguish between the two main modes of nutrition using clear definitions and examples.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students can identify autotrophs as self-feeders (plants) and heterotrophs as other-feeders (animals).",
          "layout": "comparison-table",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Autotrophic: Make own food (Plants)",
            "- Uses sunlight, water, CO2",
            "- Example: Green plants, algae",
            "Heterotrophic: Depend on others for food",
            "- No chlorophyll to make food",
            "- Example: Animals, fungi"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Autotrophs vs Heterotrophs",
            "| Feature | Autotrophs | Heterotrophs |",
            "| Source of Food | Self-made (Photosynthesis) | From others |",
            "| Example | Plants, Algae | Humans, Lions, Fungi |"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Introduce the concept of nutrition modes.",
            "Explain: Break down 'Auto' (self) and 'Hetero' (other). Use a real-life analogy: Autotrophs are like solar-powered generators; Heterotrophs are like people who buy food from stores.",
            "Ask: 'Are mushrooms autotrophs or heterotrophs?' (They eat decaying matter, so they are heterotrophs).",
            "Listen for: Confusion about fungi. Clarify that plants make their own; everything else eats something.",
            "Transition: Now let's look at how these organisms actually get energy."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "A split-screen comparison diagram. Left side: A green plant with a sun icon and arrows showing CO2/Water entering, Oxygen leaving (simplified). Right side: An animal eating or a human holding food. Use contrasting colors (Green vs Orange) to differentiate the two groups visually.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual comparison of nutrition types",
              "searchQuery": "educational diagram plant photosynthesis simple sun water carbon dioxide arrows green background flat style",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons (Public Domain)",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC0 / Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Diagram adapted for classroom use.",
              "altText": "Simplified diagram showing a plant absorbing sunlight and gases to make food.",
              "placementHint": "Left half of the slide."
            },
            {
              "purpose": "Visual comparison of nutrition types",
              "searchQuery": "educational illustration animal eating herbivore or omnivore simple flat style white background",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons (Public Domain)",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC0 / Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Diagram adapted for classroom use.",
              "altText": "Simple illustration of an animal consuming food, representing heterotrophic nutrition.",
              "placementHint": "Right half of the slide."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Left side (Autotrophs) appears first",
            "Right side (Heterotrophs) appears second"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "core_concept",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Respiration: The Energy Release Process",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 4,
          "slideType": "core_concept",
          "slideTitle": "What is Respiration?",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Respiration"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Respiration Mechanism"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Explain respiration as the process of releasing energy from food, distinct from breathing.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students understand that respiration is a chemical reaction in cells to release energy, not just inhaling/exhaling air.",
          "layout": "text-heavy-concept",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Respiration = Releasing Energy from Food",
            "- Occurs in all living cells",
            "- Requires Oxygen (Aerobic)",
            "- Produces Carbon Dioxide + Water + Energy"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Respiration: The Power Plant of the Cell",
            "| Process | Description |",
            "| Input | Food (Glucose) + Oxygen |",
            "| Output | CO2 + Water + ENERGY |",
            "| Why? | To power movement, growth, repair |"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Clarify the difference between breathing and respiration.",
            "Explain: Breathing is just moving air. Respiration is what happens inside cells to turn food into usable energy (ATP). Use a battery analogy: Food is the fuel, Oxygen is the spark, Energy is the power.",
            "Ask: 'Why do we breathe faster after running?'",
            "Listen for: Answers about needing more oxygen/energy. Correct if they say just because it's hot; emphasize energy demand.",
            "Board work: Write the word equation on board: Glucose + Oxygen -> Carbon Dioxide + Water + Energy."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "A conceptual diagram of a cell with an arrow pointing in (Oxygen/Food) and arrows pointing out (CO2/Water/Energy). Use a 'power plant' visual metaphor: gears turning or light bulbs glowing to represent energy release. Clean, schematic style.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Conceptual diagram of cellular respiration",
              "searchQuery": "educational diagram cell respiration glucose oxygen input carbon dioxide water output energy symbol flat vector white background",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons (Public Domain)",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC0 / Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Diagram adapted for classroom use.",
              "altText": "Schematic diagram showing inputs and outputs of cellular respiration within a cell context.",
              "placementHint": "Center or right side of slide."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Input arrows animate in first",
            "Output arrows follow"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "guided_practice",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Quick Check: Nutrition & Respiration",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 5,
          "slideType": "quick_assessment",
          "slideTitle": "Check Your Understanding",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Definition",
            "LO-Nutrition"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Introduction to Life Processes",
            "Nutrition Types (Autotrophic vs Heterotrophic)"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Assess student understanding of key concepts through targeted questions.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students can self-assess their grasp of autotrophs/heterotrophs and the definition of respiration.",
          "layout": "question-card",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "1. Which organism makes its own food?",
            "- A) Lion B) Rose Plant C) Human D) Mushroom",
            "2. What is released during aerobic respiration?",
            "- A) Only Oxygen B) Energy and CO2 C) Water only D) Glucose"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Quick Check: Life Processes",
            "| Question 1 | Who makes their own food? |",
            "| Answer Options | Lion, Rose Plant, Human, Mushroom |",
            "",
            "| Question 2 | What is released in respiration? |",
            "| Answer Options | Oxygen, Energy+CO2, Water only, Glucose |"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Formative assessment to check learning.",
            "Ask: 'Raise your hand if you think the Rose Plant is an autotroph.' Then ask for Question 2.",
            "Listen for: Correct identification of plants as autotrophs and energy/CO2 as respiration products.",
            "Correct: If students pick Lion, remind them lions eat other animals. If they say only Oxygen, clarify that oxygen is consumed, not released (in aerobic)."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Clean question card layout with a light background and bold text for questions. Use checkmark icons next to the correct answers that appear on click.",
          "assets": [],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal answer options one by one",
            "Show green checkmarks after discussion"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "summary",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Lesson Summary & Next Steps",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 6,
          "slideType": "recap_bridge",
          "slideTitle": "Summary: The Basics of Life",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Definition",
            "LO-Nutrition",
            "LO-Respiration"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Introduction to Life Processes",
            "Nutrition Types (Autotrophic vs Heterotrophic)",
            "Respiration Mechanism"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Summarize key takeaways and bridge to the next session.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students leave with a clear summary of what life processes are, how nutrition differs, and why respiration matters.",
          "layout": "summary-list",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Life Processes keep us alive (Nutrition, Respiration...)",
            "- Autotrophs = Self-feeders; Heterotrophs = Other-feeders",
            "- Respiration releases energy from food using oxygen"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Key Takeaways",
            "| Concept | Key Point |",
            "| Life Processes | Essential for survival (e.g., Nutrition, Respiration) |",
            "| Autotrophs | Make own food (Plants) |",
            "| Heterotrophs | Eat others (Animals/Fungi) |",
            "| Respiration | Food + O2 -> Energy + CO2 + Water |"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Consolidate learning and prepare for homework.",
            "Explain: Recap the three main points. Emphasize that respiration happens in every cell, not just lungs.",
            "Ask: 'What is one thing you learned today?'",
            "Transition: Next session we will dive deeper into how plants actually make food (Photosynthesis) and the detailed steps of breathing."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "A clean summary slide with a checklist graphic. Each key takeaway has a small icon next to it (e.g., a leaf for autotrophs, a battery for respiration).",
          "assets": [],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Bullet points appear sequentially"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 2
        }
      ]
    },
    "pdf": {
      "documentTitle": "Life Processes - Session 1: Nutrition and Respiration",
      "keyInformation": [
        "Session covers the definition of life processes, types of nutrition (autotrophic vs heterotrophic), and basic respiration.",
        "Target audience is Grade 7-9 students following a standard science curriculum.",
        "Key concepts include photosynthesis basics, aerobic respiration equation, and energy release."
      ]
    },
    "docx": {
      "outlineTitle": "Lesson Plan: Life Processes (Session 1)",
      "sections": [
        "I. Introduction to Life Processes",
        "II. Types of Nutrition: Autotrophic vs Heterotrophic",
        "III. Respiration and Energy Release",
        "IV. Assessment and Summary"
      ]
    }
  }
}
